title: A Reasonable Service
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 01-14-24 -PM- A Reasonable Service - Romans 12_1-2 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: The 'Therefore' of Romans 12
subpoints: Romans 12:1 opens with 'therefore' — the hinge connecting Romans 1–11 (doctrine) to 12–15 (application)
Chapters 1–11 establish who God is, what He has done, and how He saves — all indicatives
The first command in all of Romans (except one in chapter 6) appears here in 12:1
Everything in chapters 12–15 flows from the mercies of God declared in chapters 1–11

outline.title: First Command: Offer Your Bodies as a Living Sacrifice (v. 1)
subpoints: Old Testament sacrificial imagery: once offered, ownership transfers from the giver to God
'Body' is inclusive of the whole person — our entire life in this world given to the Lord
This sacrifice has nothing to do with justification — it is a thanksgiving offering
The sacrifice is living: vibrant, perpetually offered, not killed but alive
The sacrifice is holy: set apart for God alone, consecrated to one Master
The sacrifice is acceptable: God receives it in Christ — we need not fear rejection

outline.title: What 'Reasonable Service' Means
subpoints: Greek word for 'reasonable' (logikos) is the root of our word 'logic'
Not 'reasonable' as in a fair exchange, but as the only logical conclusion of what God has done
Wilhelmus à Brakel's four-volume systematic theology is titled A Christian's Reasonable Service — theology driving rational worship
The whole life offered to God in worship is the logical outcome of Romans 1–11

outline.title: Second Command: Stop Being Conformed to This World (v. 2a)
subpoints: The verb tense implies the Romans were already being conformed — Paul tells them to stop
The 'world' here is this present age — the culture, society, and influences that surround us
Christians are citizens of another kingdom and live by a different set of rules
We are in the world but not of it — our foundational principles necessarily clash with the world's

outline.title: Third Command: Be Transformed by the Renewal of Your Mind (v. 2b)
subpoints: The Greek word for 'transformed' (metamorphoo) is used in ancient literature for a caterpillar becoming a butterfly — total, molecular-level restructuring
This transformation is accomplished by the power of the Holy Spirit alone
2 Corinthians 3:18: we are being transformed from glory to glory into the image of Christ by the Spirit
2 Corinthians 4:16: though the outward person perishes, the inward person is renewed day by day
Martin Luther: 'The Christian life does not mean to stand still, but to move from that which is good to that which is better'
Bernard of Clairvaux: 'As soon as you do not desire to become better, you have ceased to be good'
The means of renewal: prayer, studying God's Word, thinking about God's things, talking about His Word, doing what it says

outline.title: The Goal: Proving the Good and Acceptable and Perfect Will of God (v. 2c)
subpoints: The end result of transformation is the ability to discern and approve God's will
God's declared will is morally good, acceptable, and perfect — complete
John Murray: 'If life is aimless, stagnant, fruitless, lacking content, it is because we are not entering by experience into the richness of God's will'
God's will fills every moment with meaning — there is no circumstance it does not address

transcript:
## The 'Therefore' of Romans 12

Romans 12, verses 1 and 2 are incredibly familiar verses to us. And sometimes when we are very familiar with a passage, it stops speaking to us. We have been considering Romans 12 as part of our Wednesday evening series — looking at verses 9 through 21 in the context of walking alongside one another, encouraging and loving one another. But that section is built on what Paul says in verses 1 and 2 of chapter 12. So even if you have known these scriptures by heart, even if they are your life verses, pay attention to what the Word of the Lord says here.

> "I beseech you therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God, that you present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable to God, which is your reasonable service. And do not be conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind, that you may prove what is that good and acceptable and perfect will of God." (Romans 12:1–2)

God directs us in life through His revealed Word. We sometimes get so concerned with trying to figure out God's *subjective* will — what we should do tomorrow, whom we should marry, what school we should attend — that we forget we have the objective will of God revealed in His Word. And that Word is so rich that were we to spend the rest of our lives considering it, we would not run out of things to consider.

The Apostle Paul starts with "therefore" — technically the first word, even though in Greek a sentence never begins with the conjunction *oun* ("therefore"); Paul places it first in emphasis if not in grammar. *Therefore* — I urge, I beg, I implore you to do something.

What Paul says in chapter 12 finds its foundation in chapters 1 through 11. Everything he is about to apply in chapters 12, 13, 14, and 15 rests on the doctrine he taught in the earlier chapters: God's foreordaining love; the reality of who man is apart from Christ; justification by Christ alone; the grace of sanctification; the struggles that believers face even as the Spirit indwells us; the love of God from which nothing separates us; God's eternal plans for redemption; the future of Israel and the church. All of that comes to application here in chapters 12–15 when Paul says, *Therefore, I urge you.*

It is worth noting that this is essentially the first command in the entire book of Romans — apart from one application in chapter 6. Up to this point, Paul has given us indicative after indicative: *This is who you are. This is who God is. This is what God has done for you.* And because of all these indicatives, now you are going to live in a certain way. He exhorts believers to do three things.

## First Command: Offer Your Bodies as a Living Sacrifice (v. 1)

What kind of imagery is Paul using? The imagery of the Old Testament sacrificial system — the first thing that would have come to the minds of those who heard this letter read aloud in Rome.

When an animal, grain, wine, or oil was offered to the Lord in the Old Testament, ownership over the offering transferred from the one offering to God. Once it was offered, it no longer belonged to the one who brought it — now it belonged to God. That is what Paul exhorts us to do: *offer your bodies* — give yourself to the Lord.

It is interesting that he uses the word *body*. Everyone agrees that it is inclusive — not just the physical part of our being, but the whole person. But it is significant that Paul uses *body* specifically, because this word conveys our life in this world. Paul is pleading with us to offer our lives in this world to the Lord — to make it His.

Note what kind of sacrifice this is. First, it is a **living** sacrifice. In the Old Testament, the animal was killed as it was offered. But we are living sacrifices — belonging to the Lord by the way we live, vibrant and alive, perpetually being offered to Him. Second, it is a **holy** sacrifice — set apart for God's use alone, consecrated to Him only. There is no other master we serve. Third, it is **acceptable to God**. Because of Christ, when we offer ourselves to God, He accepts us. There is nothing that can separate us from the love of God in Jesus Christ. We need not be afraid that God will somehow reject the offering of our lives, because He has already told us that in Christ He has accepted us.

## What 'Reasonable Service' Means

Why are we to offer ourselves to God as a living sacrifice? Because, Paul says, *it is your reasonable service* — your rational worship.

The word translated "reasonable" is the Greek word from which we get our word *logic*. The idea is not that our service is reasonable in the sense of a fair exchange — "God has done this for me, so it is reasonable that I do this for Him." It is not a transaction. Rather, the meaning is: *this is the only logical conclusion*. Everything that God has done for us can have only one outcome — that we worship Him with our entire lives. That is what "reasonable service" means.

Wilhelmus à Brakel, the Dutch theologian, wrote a four-volume systematic theology titled *A Christian's Reasonable Service*. His point is exactly this: theology — the truths of Romans 1–11 — is meant to propel the Christian into rational, wholehearted worship of God. Doctrine is not merely information; it is fuel for devotion.

## Second Command: Stop Being Conformed to This World (v. 2a)

> "Do not be conformed to this world." (Romans 12:2)

The way Paul writes this, the implication is that believers were already being conformed — and he tells them to *stop*. Stop being guided by this present age. Do not let this culture dictate what you do. Do not borrow your worldview from the world around you.

We are not citizens of this age or of this world, and that is where the clash happens — because we are guided by a different set of principles. Jesus said we are in the world but not of it. Paul later in Ephesians gives specifics: we are not to lie, not to steal, not to be bitter or quarrelsome, not to let corrupt speech come out of our mouths. These are all things of the world, and we are not to let them guide us.

## Third Command: Be Transformed by the Renewal of Your Mind (v. 2b)

The Greek word for "transformed" — *metamorphoo* — is used in the literature of the time for the process of a caterpillar becoming a butterfly. It is a total transformation. When the caterpillar goes into the cocoon, it completely dissolves and is restructured at the molecular level into something completely different from what it was before. That is the kind of radical transformation God calls us to in Jesus Christ.

This transformation comes by the renewal of the mind — and it is accomplished by the power of the Spirit of God. That is the only way the mind is renewed. As Paul writes in 2 Corinthians 3:18:

> "But we all, with unveiled face, beholding as in a mirror the glory of the Lord, are being transformed into the same image from glory to glory, just as by the Spirit of the Lord." (2 Corinthians 3:18)

We are transformed today — and gloriously so. Tomorrow we are transformed to a more glorious degree. There is this ascending transformation that continues until the Lord comes or we go to be with Him.

And as Paul says in 2 Corinthians 4:16:

> "Though our outward man is perishing, yet the inward man is being renewed day by day." (2 Corinthians 4:16)

As our bodies ache more with every passing year, the inward person can be growing — renewed day by day, like the young man in Isaiah 40:31, mounting up with wings as eagles.

Martin Luther said: "The Christian life does not mean to stand still, but to move from that which is good to that which is better." Bernard of Clairvaux was even more pointed: "As soon as you do not desire to become better, you have ceased to be good." The moment you say *I am where I need to be; I have arrived; I don't need any more growth* — at that moment you have ceased to be any good, because the Christian is moving from glory to glory by the work of the Spirit, becoming more and more like the Lord Jesus Christ.

The means God gives us to renew our minds are the means of grace: prayer, studying the Word of God, thinking about the things of God, talking about God's Word, and doing what the Word of God says. That is what the rest of chapter 12 and chapters 13, 14, and 15 describe.

## The Goal: Proving the Good and Perfect Will of God (v. 2c)

The end of all of this is stated at the close of verse 2: *that you may prove what is that good and acceptable and perfect will of God.* The result of this transformation is the ability to discern, approve, and discover God's declared will — His morally good, acceptable, and perfect will.

John Murray, the Westminster Theological Seminary professor, put it well: "If life is aimless, stagnant, fruitless, lacking content, it is because we are not entering by experience into the richness of God's will. The commandment of God is exceeding broad. There is not a moment of life that the will of God does not command. No circumstance that it does not fill with meaning, if we are responsive to the fullness of His revealed counsel for us."

And it is because of that — because God's will fills every moment with meaning — that Paul says: *I beseech you therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God, that you present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable to God, which is your reasonable service; and do not be conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind, that you may prove what is that good and acceptable and perfect will of God.*
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro expounds Romans 12:1–2, the 'therefore' that connects the doctrinal foundation of Romans 1–11 to the practical application of chapters 12–15. He unpacks the three commands: offer your bodies as a living, holy, and acceptable sacrifice (a total transfer of ownership to God); stop being conformed to this present age; and be transformed by the renewal of the mind — a Spirit-wrought, progressive, caterpillar-to-butterfly metamorphosis that enables believers to discern and do the good, acceptable, and perfect will of God.
